questions.source_ref: Section 8.1: Indivisible Items: Model and Applications
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the indivisible goods allocation model and its applications? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Indivisible goods allocation is a combinatorial optimisation problem because items cannot be split into fractions
Typical applications include inheritance division, task allocation, dormitory room assignment, and computing resource distribution
In the formal model, valuations are functions $v_i : G \to \mathbb{R}$ defined only on single goods
A complete allocation requires the bundles to be pairwise disjoint and their union to equal $G$
An allocation is partial whenever some good is assigned to more than one agent

explanations.text: The notes explicitly identify indivisible allocation as a combinatorial optimisation problem because goods cannot be fractionally divided.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.1: Indivisible Items: Model and Applications
explanations.notes_quote: Finding an optimal allocation is a combinatorial optimization problem because items cannot be divided as fractions

explanations.text: All four examples are listed as real-world applications in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.1: Indivisible Items: Model and Applications
explanations.notes_quote: Dividing inheritance items among heirs; Allocating tasks among team members; Assigning dormitory rooms to students; Distributing computing resources in data centres

explanations.text: The formal model uses set valuations on bundles: $v_i : 2^G \to \mathbb{R}$ (or $\mathbb{Z}$ computationally), not only on single goods.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.1: Indivisible Items: Model and Applications

explanations.text: Completeness and disjointness are both part of the allocation definition.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.1: Indivisible Items: Model and Applications
explanations.notes_quote: $A_i \cap A_j = \emptyset$ for $i \neq j$ ... Allocation is complete if $\bigcup_{i \in N} A_i = G$

explanations.text: A partial allocation means not all goods are allocated. Assigning one good to multiple agents would violate the partition requirement rather than define partiality.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.1: Indivisible Items: Model and Applications

questions.source_ref: Section 8.1: Indivisible Items: Model and Applications
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe additive valuations and allocation structure in the formal model? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Additivity means $v_i(S) = \sum_{g \in S} v_i(g)$ for all $S \subseteq G$
The model assumes $v_i(\emptyset) = 0$
If valuations are additive, then every complete allocation is automatically envy-free
A bundle $A_i$ is the subset of goods allocated to agent $i$
Additivity requires every agent to assign the same value to each good

explanations.text: This is the exact definition of additive valuations given in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.1: Indivisible Items: Model and Applications
explanations.notes_quote: Additive valuations: $v_i(S) = \sum_{g \in S} v_i(g)$

explanations.text: The valuation normalisation for the empty bundle is stated explicitly.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.1: Indivisible Items: Model and Applications
explanations.notes_quote: $v_i(\emptyset) = 0$

explanations.text: Additivity alone does not imply envy-freeness; the notes later introduce EF1 precisely because exact fairness is often infeasible.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.2: Solution Concepts and Relaxations (PROP1 and EF1)

explanations.text: The allocation is written as $A = (A_1, \ldots, A_n)$ where each $A_i$ is the bundle assigned to agent $i$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.1: Indivisible Items: Model and Applications
explanations.notes_quote: $A_i \subseteq G$ is the bundle allocated to agent $i$

explanations.text: Additivity concerns how bundle values decompose into item values for each agent; it does not require identical valuations across agents.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.1: Indivisible Items: Model and Applications

sections.title: 8.2 Solution Concepts and Relaxations (PROP1 and EF1)

questions.source_ref: Section 8.2: Basic Solution Concepts
questions.text: Which of the following correctly define the basic fairness and efficiency concepts for indivisible goods? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Proportionality requires $v_i(A_i) \geq v_i(G)/n$ for every agent $i$
Envy-freeness requires $v_i(A_i) \geq v_i(A_j)$ for all agents $i,j$
Equitability requires every agent to receive the same bundle of goods
Pareto-optimality means there is no other allocation making everyone weakly better off and someone strictly better off
An equitable allocation satisfies $v_i(A_i) = v_j(A_j)$ for all $i,j$

explanations.text: This is the proportionality definition stated in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Proportional if $v_i(A_i) \geq v_i(G)/n$ for all $i \in N$

explanations.text: This is the exact envy-free condition.
explanations.notes_quote: Envy-free (EF) if $v_i(A_i) \geq v_i(A_j)$ for all $i, j \in N$

explanations.text: Equitability requires equal achieved utility, not identical bundles. Different bundles may still be equitable if agents value them equally.
This matches the Pareto-optimality definition in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: there is no other allocation $B = (B_1, \ldots, B_n)$ such that $v_i(B_i) \geq v_i(A_i)$ for all $i \in N$ and $v_i(B_i) > v_i(A_i)$ for some $i \in N$

explanations.text: Equitability is defined by equality of attained utilities across agents.
explanations.notes_quote: Equitable if $v_i(A_i) = v_j(A_j)$ for all $i, j \in N$

questions.source_ref: Section 8.2: The Core Challenge; Section 8.2: Relaxations: PROP1 and EF1
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe why relaxations such as PROP1 and EF1 are needed? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: With indivisible goods, exact envy-freeness and proportionality are generally infeasible
A counterexample is 2 agents and 1 positively valued item, where no proportional, envy-free, or equitable allocation exists
The infeasibility disappears once the allocation is complete
Unlike cake cutting, indivisible goods cannot simply be divided into fractions to satisfy exact fairness
PROP1 and EF1 are introduced as weaker notions that can still hold when exact fairness fails

explanations.text: The notes state this as the central challenge for indivisible goods.
explanations.notes_quote: With indivisible goods, envy-freeness and proportionality are generally infeasible

explanations.text: This is the explicit counterexample used in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Counterexample: 2 agents, 1 item, both assign positive value -- no proportional, envy-free, or equitable allocation exists

explanations.text: Completeness alone does not restore exact fairness. The notes later show complete EF1 allocations that are still not proportional for general valuations.
The contrast with divisible settings is stated directly.
explanations.notes_quote: Unlike divisible settings (cake cutting), we cannot simply divide each good

explanations.text: These notions are introduced precisely as relaxations of exact fairness.
explanations.notes_quote: Relaxations: PROP1 and EF1

questions.source_ref: Section 8.2: Relaxations: PROP1 and EF1
questions.text: Which of the following correctly define PROP1 and EF1? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: PROP1 means that for each agent $i$ there exists an item $g \in G$ such that $v_i(A_i \cup \{g\}) \geq v_i(G)/n$
EF1 means that for each pair $i,j$ there exists an item $g \in A_j$ such that $v_i(A_i) \geq v_i(A_j \setminus \{g\})$
EF1 requires the same single item $g$ to work simultaneously for every pair of agents
Under PROP1, each agent can reach their proportional share by adding at most one extra good to their bundle
In the 2-agent, 1-house example, giving the house to one agent is both PROP1 and EF1

explanations.text: This is the formal PROP1 definition from the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: An allocation $A = (A_1, \ldots, A_n)$ is proportional up to 1 good (PROP1) if for each agent $i$ there is an item $g \in G$ such that: $v_i(A_i \cup \{g\}) \geq v_i(G)/n$

explanations.text: This is the exact EF1 condition.
explanations.notes_quote: An allocation $A = (A_1, \ldots, A_n)$ is envy-free up to 1 good (EF1) if for each pair of agents $i, j$ there is an item $g \in A_j$ such that: $v_i(A_i) \geq v_i(A_j \setminus \{g\})$

explanations.text: EF1 only requires existence of some suitable item for each ordered pair $(i,j)$; it need not be the same item across all pairs.
This is the intuition given immediately after the PROP1 definition.
explanations.notes_quote: Each agent could reach their proportional $1/n$-th share by adding at most one extra good to their bundle

explanations.text: The notes use the one-house example to illustrate both relaxations.
explanations.notes_quote: Example: 2 agents, 1 house. Giving the house to one agent is both PROP1 and EF1

questions.source_ref: Section 8.2: PROP1 vs. EF1
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the relationship between EF1 and PROP1? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: For general valuations, EF1 plus completeness does not necessarily imply PROP1
The counterexample for general valuations gives each agent one item, yielding EF and completeness but still not PROP1
For additive valuations, EF1 and completeness imply PROP1
In the additive proof, one chooses for agent $i$ a most valuable item $g^*$ in $G \setminus A_i$
The additive proof concludes PROP1 by showing $n \times v_i(A_i) \geq v_i(G)$ directly, without adding any item

explanations.text: The notes explicitly say the implication fails for general valuations.
explanations.notes_quote: NO for general valuations

explanations.text: That is exactly how the counterexample is described: the allocation is EF and complete, yet adding one item still leaves value 0 for each agent.
explanations.notes_quote: Giving each agent one item is EF (hence EF1) and complete, but not PROP1 because adding one item to any bundle still gives value 0

explanations.text: The notes prove this implication for additive valuations.
explanations.notes_quote: YES for additive valuations

explanations.text: The proof begins by selecting the most valuable item outside $A_i$.
explanations.notes_quote: Consider agent $i$; let $g^*$ be their most valuable item in $G \setminus A_i$

explanations.text: The proof first augments $A_i$ with $g^*$ and derives $n \times v_i(A_i \cup \{g^*\}) \geq v_i(G)$, not the stronger statement without adding an item.

questions.source_ref: Section 8.3: The Algorithm
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the Round-Robin algorithm? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Agents take turns choosing their most preferred remaining item
Agent $i$ chooses in rounds $i, n+i, 2n+i, \ldots$
Round-Robin is defined for arbitrary monotone valuations because it only compares bundles
The algorithm is presented for additive valuations
Round-Robin requires all agents to pick simultaneously in each round

explanations.text: This is the core rule of the algorithm.
explanations.notes_quote: Agents take turns choosing their most preferred item among the remaining items

explanations.text: The notes specify the turn structure exactly this way.
explanations.notes_quote: Agent $i$ chooses in rounds $i, n+i, 2n+i, \ldots$

explanations.text: The notes later state Round-Robin is not even defined for non-additive valuations because it relies on evaluating individual items.
The section title and theorem both restrict RR to additive valuations.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.3: Round-Robin Algorithm for Additive Valuations
explanations.notes_quote: Round-Robin Algorithm for Additive Valuations

explanations.text: Agents pick sequentially in a fixed order, not simultaneously.

questions.source_ref: Section 8.3: Worked Example (from slides)
questions.text:
Given the Round-Robin valuation table from the slides:

|       | $g_1$ | $g_2$ | $g_3$ | $g_4$ | $g_5$ |
|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| $a_1$ | 20    | 50    | 12    | 45    | 3     |
| $a_2$ | 35    | 22    | 90    | 40    | 4     |
| $a_3$ | 30    | 80    | 7     | 18    | 25    |

with order $a_1 \to a_2 \to a_3$, which of the following statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In round 1, $a_1$ picks $g_2$, $a_2$ picks $g_3$, and $a_3$ picks $g_1$
In round 2, $a_1$ picks $g_4$ and $a_2$ picks $g_5$
The final allocation is $A_1 = \{g_2, g_4\}$, $A_2 = \{g_3, g_5\}$, $A_3 = \{g_1\}$
$a_3$ receives two items because $a_3$ values $g_2$ at 80
$a_2$'s total value for their bundle is 94

explanations.text: These are exactly the first-round picks listed in the worked example.
explanations.notes_quote: Round 1: $a_1$ picks $g_2$ (value 50), $a_2$ picks $g_3$ (value 90), $a_3$ picks $g_1$ (value 30)

explanations.text: These are the second-round picks after the first three items are removed.
explanations.notes_quote: Round 2: $a_1$ picks $g_4$ (value 45), $a_2$ picks $g_5$ (value 4)

explanations.text: This is the final allocation reported in the notes.
$a_3$ receives only one item because there are five goods and the order ends after $a_2$'s second pick. Also $g_2$ is already taken by $a_1$ in round 1.
$a_2$ gets $g_3$ and $g_5$, worth $90 + 4 = 94$ to $a_2$.
explanations.notes_quote: $a_2$ picks $g_3$ (value 90) ... $a_2$ picks $g_5$ (value 4)

questions.source_ref: Section 8.3: Worked Example (from LGT notes)
questions.text:
Given the LGT Round-Robin example:

|       | A  | B  | C  | D  |
|-------|----|----|----|----|
| $v_1$ | 9  | 5  | 4  | 1  |
| $v_2$ | 6  | 7  | 5  | 2  |
| $v_3$ | 3  | 2  | 8  | 10 |

with order $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 1$, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Agent 1 first picks A, agent 2 picks B, and agent 3 picks D
Agent 1's second pick is C
The final allocation is $A_1 = \{A, C\}$, $A_2 = \{B\}$, $A_3 = \{D\}$
Agent 1's value for their final bundle is 14
Agent 3's value for their final bundle is 10

explanations.text: These are the first three picks in the example.
explanations.notes_quote: $a_1$ picks A (value 9), $a_2$ picks B (value 7), $a_3$ picks D (value 10)

explanations.text: After A, B, and D are taken, agent 1 picks C.
explanations.notes_quote: $a_1$ picks C (value 4)

explanations.text: This is the final allocation stated in the notes.
Agent 1 values $A$ and $C$ at $9 + 4 = 13$, not 14.
Agent 3 receives D, which they value at 10.

questions.source_ref: Section 8.3: Theorem: Round-Robin Outputs EF1 (and hence PROP1) for Additive Valuations
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe why Round-Robin outputs EF1 for additive valuations? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: If $i < j$, then each item picked by $i$ is at least as good for $i$ as the corresponding item picked later by $j$
Therefore, when $i < j$, agent $i$ does not envy agent $j$
If $j < i$, removing $i$'s first item is enough to eliminate $j$'s envy of $i$
The proof relies on additivity to extend per-round comparisons across the whole bundle
The theorem proves Round-Robin is always Pareto-optimal because no envy remains

explanations.text: This is the first step of the proof for the case where $i$ picks before $j$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.3: Theorem: Round-Robin Outputs EF1 (and hence PROP1) for Additive Valuations
explanations.notes_quote: $i$'s first item is no worse to $i$ than $j$'s first item ... $i$'s second item is no worse to $i$ than $j$'s second item

explanations.text: The notes conclude from the per-round comparison that $i$ does not envy $j$.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.3: Theorem: Round-Robin Outputs EF1 (and hence PROP1) for Additive Valuations
explanations.notes_quote: Therefore, $i$ does not envy $j$

explanations.text: This is the second case in the proof when the other agent picked earlier.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.3: Theorem: Round-Robin Outputs EF1 (and hence PROP1) for Additive Valuations
explanations.notes_quote: If $j < i$ ... remove $i$'s first item ... $j$ does not envy $i$ after removing $i$'s first item

explanations.text: The proof explicitly invokes additivity to aggregate the round-by-round inequalities.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.3: Theorem: Round-Robin Outputs EF1 (and hence PROP1) for Additive Valuations
explanations.notes_quote: Since valuations are additive, these per-round claims extend across rounds

explanations.text: The theorem only establishes EF1, and the notes later give a counterexample showing RR is not Pareto-optimal.

questions.source_ref: Section 8.3: Limitations of Round-Robin
questions.text:
Consider the Round-Robin Pareto-optimality counterexample:

| Item | Agent 1 | Agent 2 |
|------|---------|---------|
| 1    | 10      | 100     |
| 2    | 9       | 3       |
| 3    | 8       | 2       |
| 4    | 7       | 1       |

Which of the following statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Round-Robin gives items 1 and 3 to agent 1, and items 2 and 4 to agent 2
Under that RR allocation, agent 1 gets utility 18 and agent 2 gets utility 4
A trade can make both agents strictly better off, so the RR allocation is not Pareto-optimal
The example shows RR is not strategyproof rather than not Pareto-optimal
The notes use this example to show that EF1 implies Pareto-optimality for additive valuations

explanations.text: This is the RR outcome stated in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: RR gives: items 1, 3 to agent 1 ... items 2, 4 to agent 2

explanations.text: The utilities are explicitly given as 18 and 4.
explanations.notes_quote: RR gives: items 1, 3 to agent 1 (utility 18); items 2, 4 to agent 2 (utility 4)

explanations.text: The improving trade demonstrates a Pareto improvement, so the RR allocation is not PO.
explanations.notes_quote: Improving trade ... Both improve

explanations.text: This specific table is used for the non-PO counterexample. Strategyproofness is shown by a different table later in the notes.
The notes say the opposite: RR is EF1, but not Pareto-optimal.

questions.source_ref: Section 8.3: Limitations of Round-Robin
questions.text:
Consider the Round-Robin strategyproofness counterexample:

| Item | Agent 1 | Agent 2 |
|------|---------|---------|
| 1    | 9       | 1       |
| 2    | 8       | 9       |
| 3    | 2       | 2       |
| 4    | 1       | 3       |

Which of the following statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Under truthful Round-Robin, agent 1 gets items 1 and 3 for utility 11
If agent 1 strategically picks item 2 first, they can later also get item 1
Under that manipulation, agent 1's utility rises from 11 to 17
This shows Round-Robin is not strategyproof, even for additive valuations
The manipulation works because agent 2 is forced to take item 3 after item 2 is removed

explanations.text: This is the truthful outcome described in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Truthful RR: agent 1 gets items 1, 3 (utility 11)

explanations.text: The notes describe exactly this profitable deviation.
explanations.notes_quote: If agent 1 picks item 2 first (misreporting preferences): agent 2 takes item 4, then agent 1 takes item 1

explanations.text: The manipulated bundle gives agent 1 utility 17 instead of 11.
explanations.notes_quote: Agent 1 gets items 1, 2 (utility 17 vs. 11)

explanations.text: This is the conclusion drawn from the example.
explanations.notes_quote: Not strategyproof, even for additive valuations

explanations.text: The notes say agent 2 takes item 4, not item 3, after agent 1 grabs item 2 first.

sections.title: 8.4 Envy-Cycle Elimination Algorithm for Monotone Valuations

questions.source_ref: Section 8.4: Motivation; Section 8.4: Key Idea: Extending a Partial EF1 Allocation
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the motivation and key idea behind Envy-Cycle Elimination (ECE)? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Round-Robin is not even defined for non-additive valuations because it relies on evaluating individual items
ECE targets monotone valuations, where $S \subseteq T$ implies $v_i(S) \leq v_i(T)$
If an agent is unenvied, giving them an unallocated item preserves EF1
If every agent is envied by someone, EF1 is impossible and the algorithm must terminate unsuccessfully
The notes ask whether EF1 can still be achieved for monotone valuations

explanations.text: This is the explicit motivation for moving beyond RR.
explanations.notes_quote: Round-robin is not even defined for non-additive valuations (it relies on evaluating individual items)

explanations.text: This is the monotonicity condition stated in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Monotone valuations: For all $S \subseteq T \subseteq G$ and every agent $i$: $v_i(S) \leq v_i(T)$

explanations.text: The key extension lemma says an unenvied agent can safely receive an unallocated item while preserving EF1.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.4: Key Idea: Extending a Partial EF1 Allocation
explanations.notes_quote: If there is an agent $i$ whom nobody envies, we can safely give $i$ an unallocated item and preserve EF1

explanations.text: If every agent is envied by someone, the notes introduce envy cycles and RAC steps rather than failure.
This is the motivating question posed in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Can we achieve EF1 for monotone valuations?

questions.source_ref: Section 8.4: The Envy Graph; Section 8.4: Worked Example (Envy Graph)
questions.text: Given the envy-graph example allocation $A_1 = \{g_1, g_5\}$, $A_2 = \{g_2, g_3\}$, $A_3 = \{g_4\}$, which of the following statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The envy graph has a directed edge from $i$ to $j$ when $v_i(A_i) < v_i(A_j)$
In the worked example, $a_1$ envies $a_2$, $a_2$ envies $a_3$, and $a_3$ envies $a_1$
These envy relations form a cycle $a_1 \to a_2 \to a_3 \to a_1$
Because the envy graph contains a cycle, the allocation cannot be EF1
The notes state that the allocation is EF1 because removing one item from each envied bundle eliminates envy

explanations.text: This is the definition of an envy edge.
explanations.notes_quote: Directed edge from $i$ to $j$ if $i$ envies $j$ (i.e., $v_i(A_i) < v_i(A_j)$)

explanations.text: These three envy relations are listed explicitly in the example.
explanations.notes_quote: $a_1$ envies $a_2$ ... $a_2$ envies $a_3$ ... $a_3$ envies $a_1$

explanations.text: The notes identify this as an envy cycle.
explanations.notes_quote: This forms an envy cycle: $a_1 \to a_2 \to a_3 \to a_1$

explanations.text: The notes explicitly say the allocation is EF1 even though the envy graph contains a cycle.
This is the explanation given for why the example allocation is EF1.
explanations.notes_quote: The allocation is EF1 (removing one item from each envied bundle eliminates envy)

questions.source_ref: Section 8.4: Reallocation Along a Cycle (RAC); Section 8.4: Bounding the Number of RAC Steps
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe reallocation along a cycle (RAC) and its analysis? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In RAC, each agent on a cycle receives the next agent's bundle along the cycle
RAC preserves EF1 because the set of bundles does not change
Every agent on the cycle receives a strictly more preferred bundle
The total score used in the runtime argument strictly decreases after each RAC step
The notes bound the number of consecutive RAC steps by at most $n(n-1)$

explanations.text: This is exactly how RAC is defined.
explanations.notes_quote: Give $A_j$ to $i$, $A_k$ to $j$, ..., $A_i$ to $t$

explanations.text: The notes say EF1 is preserved because the set of bundles remains the same.
explanations.notes_quote: The set of bundles does not change, so the allocation remains EF1

explanations.text: Each agent on the cycle strictly prefers the bundle they receive after RAC.
explanations.notes_quote: Each agent on the cycle receives a strictly more preferred bundle

explanations.text: The score strictly increases after each RAC step; that increase is what yields the termination bound.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.4: Bounding the Number of RAC Steps
explanations.notes_quote: Therefore strictly increases the total score

explanations.text: This is the explicit bound proved in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.4: Bounding the Number of RAC Steps
explanations.notes_quote: At most $n(n-1)$ consecutive RAC steps before termination

questions.source_ref: Section 8.4: The ECE Algorithm
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the ECE algorithm and its runtime? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: ECE starts from the empty allocation, which is trivially EF1
Whenever an unenvied agent exists, ECE allocates an unallocated item to that agent
If no unenvied agent exists, the notes argue there must be an envy cycle and RAC can be applied
The runtime bound stated in the notes is $O(m \cdot n^2)$ steps
ECE guarantees a Pareto-optimal allocation for all monotone valuations

explanations.text: This is the first step of the algorithm.
explanations.notes_quote: Start with an empty allocation $A$ (which is trivially EF1)

explanations.text: This is the main allocation step when an unenvied agent is available.
explanations.notes_quote: If there is an unenvied agent, allocate them an unallocated item ($A$ remains EF1)

explanations.text: The notes present a dichotomy: either there is a cycle or there is an unenvied agent.
explanations.notes_quote: Otherwise, there must be an envy cycle -- perform an RAC step

explanations.text: The polynomial runtime is stated explicitly.
explanations.notes_quote: Total: $O(m \cdot n^2)$ steps

explanations.text: The notes later give a counterexample showing ECE is not Pareto-optimal, even with additive valuations.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.4: ECE Is Also Not Pareto-Optimal

questions.source_ref: Section 8.4: ECE Is Also Not Pareto-Optimal; Section 8.4: ECE Worked Example (from LGT notes)
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the ECE examples in the notes? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The additive counterexample with items 1 to 4 shows ECE can fail to be Pareto-optimal because swapping items 3 and 4 makes both agents strictly better off
In the LGT worked example, an envy cycle arises between $a_2$ and $a_3$ after bundles $\{C\}$ and $\{B\}$ are assigned
The RAC step in the LGT example swaps the bundles of $a_2$ and $a_3$
The final ECE allocation in the LGT example differs from the Round-Robin outcome
The notes say the final ECE allocation in that example is $A_1 = \{A, C\}$, $A_2 = \{B\}$, $A_3 = \{D\}$

explanations.text: This is the improving trade used to show ECE is not PO.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.4: ECE Is Also Not Pareto-Optimal
explanations.notes_quote: Improving trade: swap items 3 and 4 -- both agents strictly improve

explanations.text: The notes explicitly identify an envy cycle between agents 2 and 3 at that stage.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.4: ECE Worked Example (from LGT notes)
explanations.notes_quote: Envy cycle between $a_2$ and $a_3$

explanations.text: The RAC step swaps those two bundles.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.4: ECE Worked Example (from LGT notes)
explanations.notes_quote: RAC: swap bundles of $a_2$ and $a_3$: $A_2 = \{B\}$, $A_3 = \{C\}$

explanations.text: The notes say the final ECE allocation is the same as RR in this case.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.4: ECE Worked Example (from LGT notes)
explanations.notes_quote: Final: $A_1 = \{A, C\}$, $A_2 = \{B\}$, $A_3 = \{D\}$ (same as RR in this case)

explanations.text: This is the final allocation reported in the worked example.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.4: ECE Worked Example (from LGT notes)

questions.source_ref: Section 8.5: Fairness vs. Efficiency Summary; Section 8.5: Three Welfare Concepts
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the welfare concepts and the fairness-efficiency summary? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Utilitarian welfare is $UW(A) = \sum_{i \in N} v_i(A_i)$
Egalitarian welfare is $EW(A) = \min_{i \in N} v_i(A_i)$
Nash welfare is $NW(A) = \prod_{i \in N} v_i(A_i)$
The notes state that EF1 plus Pareto-optimality can always be guaranteed simultaneously in the general case
The notes summarise that EF1 always exists for additive valuations via RR and for monotone valuations via ECE

explanations.text: This is the utilitarian welfare definition.
explanations.notes_quote: $UW(A) = \sum_{i \in N} v_i(A_i)$

explanations.text: This is the egalitarian welfare definition.
explanations.notes_quote: $EW(A) = \min_{i \in N} v_i(A_i)$

explanations.text: This is the Nash welfare definition.
explanations.notes_quote: $NW(A) = \prod_{i \in N} v_i(A_i)$

explanations.text: The notes say the opposite: EF1 and PO cannot be simultaneously guaranteed in the general case.
explanations.notes_quote: EF1 + PO cannot be simultaneously guaranteed in the general case

explanations.text: This is part of the summary at the start of the section.
explanations.notes_quote: EF1: Always exists for additive (Round-Robin) and monotone (ECE) valuations

questions.source_ref: Section 8.5: Worked Example
questions.text: For the welfare example with five goods where $a_1$ values every item at 10, $a_2$ values every item at 1, and $a_3$ values every item at 3, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Maximising utilitarian welfare gives all five items to $a_1$, yielding $UW = 50$
The notes describe the utilitarian optimum as highly unfair
A max-egalitarian allocation can give 3 items to $a_2$, 1 to $a_1$, and 1 to $a_3$, yielding $EW = 3$
A max-Nash allocation gives one item to each agent and leaves two items unallocated
The notes state a max-Nash welfare value of 120 in this example

explanations.text: This is the utilitarian optimum reported in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Max Utilitarian welfare: Give all 5 items to $a_1$ $\Rightarrow UW = 50$

explanations.text: The notes explicitly comment on its unfairness.
explanations.notes_quote: Highly unfair

explanations.text: This is the egalitarian optimum described in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Max Egalitarian welfare: Give 3 items to $a_2$, 1 to $a_1$, 1 to $a_3$ $\Rightarrow EW = 3$

explanations.text: The notes say the max-Nash allocation gives 2 items each to two agents and 1 item to the third, not that items are left unallocated.
explanations.notes_quote: 2 agents get 2 items each, 1 gets 1 item

explanations.text: The stated Nash welfare value is 120.
explanations.notes_quote: Max Nash welfare ... $NW = 120$

questions.source_ref: Section 8.5: Worked Example (from LGT notes); Section 8.5: Properties of Welfare Maximisers
questions.text: Using the LGT valuation table for items A, B, C, D, which of the following statements are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A utilitarian welfare maximiser allocates A to agent 1, B to agent 2, and C,D to agent 3, for total welfare 34
An egalitarian welfare maximiser in the notes is $A_1 = \{A\}$, $A_2 = \{B, C\}$, $A_3 = \{D\}$, with minimum utility 9
The Nash welfare maximiser in the notes is the same allocation as the utilitarian maximiser
The notes state that utilitarian maximisers are always Pareto-optimal
The notes state that egalitarian welfare maximisers are always EF1

explanations.text: This is the utilitarian optimum computed in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: Max Utilitarian welfare: Allocate each item to the agent who values it most: $A_1 = \{A\}$ (9), $A_2 = \{B\}$ (7), $A_3 = \{C, D\}$ (18). $\sum = 34$

explanations.text: This is the egalitarian optimum listed in the notes.
The notes give the same allocation for the Nash optimum in this example.
This is one of the stated properties of welfare maximisers.
explanations.notes_quote: Utilitarian welfare maximiser is PO

explanations.text: The notes explicitly say egalitarian welfare maximisers are not necessarily EF1.

questions.source_ref: Section 8.5: Properties of Welfare Maximisers
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the properties of utilitarian, egalitarian, and Nash welfare maximisers? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Any Pareto improvement to a utilitarian welfare maximiser would increase the sum of utilities, contradicting maximality
The notes give a counterexample showing an egalitarian welfare maximiser need not be Pareto-optimal
For additive valuations, a Nash welfare maximiser is always Pareto-optimal and EF1
The EF1 guarantee for Nash welfare maximisers is proved in full detail in the notes
The notes say utilitarian welfare maximisers may be highly unfair

explanations.text: This is the proof idea given for utilitarian Pareto-optimality.
explanations.notes_quote: Any Pareto improvement would increase the sum, contradicting maximality

explanations.text: The notes provide a 2-agent, 3-item example showing egalitarian maximality need not imply PO.
explanations.notes_quote: Egalitarian welfare maximiser is NOT necessarily PO

explanations.text: This is the headline property of Nash welfare maximisers for additive valuations.
explanations.notes_quote: Nash welfare maximiser gives both PO and EF1 (for additive valuations)

explanations.text: The notes explicitly say the EF1 proof for Nash welfare is non-trivial and beyond course scope.
explanations.notes_quote: EF1: Proof is non-trivial (beyond course scope)

explanations.text: This is stated both in the summary and in the worked example discussion.
explanations.notes_quote: Utilitarian welfare maximiser is always PO but can be unfair

questions.source_ref: Section 8.6: Definition; Section 8.6: EF1 vs. EFX vs. EF
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe EFX and its relation to EF1 and EF? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: EFX requires that for each pair of agents $i,j$ and for each item $g \in A_j$, we have $v_i(A_i) \geq v_i(A_j \setminus \{g\})$
EF implies EFX, and EFX implies EF1
EF1 requires envy to disappear after removing every item from the envied bundle
EFX is strictly stronger than EF1 and strictly weaker than EF
Under EF, no envy exists at all, so no item removal is needed

explanations.text: This is the formal definition of EFX.
explanations.notes_quote: An allocation $A = (A_1, \ldots, A_n)$ is envy-free up to any good (EFX) if for each pair of agents $i, j \in N$ and for each item $g \in A_j$: $v_i(A_i) \geq v_i(A_j \setminus \{g\})$

explanations.text: The implication chain is stated explicitly.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.6: EF1 vs. EFX vs. EF
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{EF} \implies \text{EFX} \implies \text{EF1}$

explanations.text: That is EFX, not EF1. EF1 only requires existence of some item whose removal eliminates envy.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.6: EF1 vs. EFX vs. EF

explanations.text: The notes state this comparison directly.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.6: EF1 vs. EFX vs. EF
explanations.notes_quote: EFX is strictly stronger than EF1 and strictly weaker than EF

explanations.text: This is the interpretation of exact envy-freeness given in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.6: EF1 vs. EFX vs. EF
explanations.notes_quote: EF: No envy exists at all (no removal needed)

questions.source_ref: Section 8.6: Existence of EFX (Open Problem); Section 8.6: EFX for Identical Additive Valuations
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the existence status of EFX and the identical-valuations result? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: For 2 agents with monotone valuations, EFX existence is known to be true
For 3 agents with additive valuations, EFX existence is known to be true
For $n \geq 4$ agents with additive valuations, EFX existence is listed as an open problem
For identical additive valuations and any number of agents, EFX allocations are guaranteed to exist
The algorithm for identical additive valuations sorts items by increasing value and gives each item to the currently most satisfied agent

explanations.text: This is one row of the existence table.
explanations.notes_quote: 2 agents, monotone valuations | Yes

explanations.text: This is another row of the existence table.
explanations.notes_quote: 3 agents, additive valuations | Yes

explanations.text: The notes identify this as the major open case.
explanations.notes_quote: $n \geq 4$ agents, additive valuations | Open problem

explanations.text: The theorem states existence and polynomial-time computability for identical additive valuations.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.6: EFX for Identical Additive Valuations
explanations.notes_quote: EFX allocations are guaranteed to exist (and can be found in polynomial time) if all agents have identical additive valuations

explanations.text: The algorithm sorts items by decreasing value and gives each item to the currently least satisfied agent.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.6: EFX for Identical Additive Valuations

questions.source_ref: Section 8.6: EFX for Identical Additive Valuations
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the algorithm and proof idea for EFX under identical additive valuations? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The algorithm sorts items by decreasing value
Each next item is given to the currently least satisfied agent
In the proof, if agent $i$ envies agent $j$, the last item received by $j$ is used as the key comparison item
The proof argues that when $j$ received that last item, agent $i$ was at least as satisfied as agent $j$
The proof concludes only EF1, not EFX, because the smallest item may fail for some agents

explanations.text: This is the first step of the algorithm.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.6: EFX for Identical Additive Valuations
explanations.notes_quote: Sort items by decreasing value

explanations.text: This is the allocation rule in the algorithm.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.6: EFX for Identical Additive Valuations
explanations.notes_quote: Allocate one by one, giving each item to the currently least satisfied agent

explanations.text: The proof focuses on the last item received by the envied agent, which is their smallest item.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.6: EFX for Identical Additive Valuations
explanations.notes_quote: Let $g$ be the last item received by $j$ (this is $j$'s smallest item)

explanations.text: This is the central invariant used in the proof.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.6: EFX for Identical Additive Valuations
explanations.notes_quote: When $j$ received $g$, agent $i$ was at least as satisfied as agent $j$

explanations.text: The proof concludes EFX by showing $v_i(A_i) \geq v_i(A_j \setminus \{g\})$ for the relevant smallest item, not merely EF1.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.6: EFX for Identical Additive Valuations
explanations.notes_quote: Hence $v_i(A_i) \geq v_i(A_j \setminus \{g\})$

questions.source_ref: Section Exercises and Solutions (SGT8): Exercise 1: Nash Welfare Invariance Under Scaling
questions.text: Which of the following correctly explain why scaling one agent's valuation by 10 preserves the Nash welfare maximiser? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: If $v'_i(g) = 10 \cdot v_i(g)$, then every allocation's Nash welfare is multiplied by the same factor 10
Because all allocations are scaled by the same constant factor, the argmax does not change
The argument relies on additivity when passing from item values to bundle values
Scaling one agent's valuation changes the ranking of allocations because only one term in the product changes
The notes conclude that the original allocation still maximises Nash welfare after the scaling

explanations.text: This is the core observation in the solution.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises and Solutions (SGT8): Exercise 1: Nash Welfare Invariance Under Scaling
explanations.notes_quote: scaling agent $i$'s valuation multiplies the Nash welfare by exactly 10

explanations.text: If every candidate objective value is multiplied by the same positive constant, the maximiser is unchanged.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises and Solutions (SGT8): Exercise 1: Nash Welfare Invariance Under Scaling
explanations.notes_quote: Since all allocations' Nash welfare is scaled by the same factor, the maximiser does not change

explanations.text: The notes explicitly mention additivity in the justification.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises and Solutions (SGT8): Exercise 1: Nash Welfare Invariance Under Scaling
explanations.notes_quote: by additivity

explanations.text: Although one factor changes, it changes by the same multiplicative constant for every allocation, so the ranking does not change.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises and Solutions (SGT8): Exercise 1: Nash Welfare Invariance Under Scaling

explanations.text: That is the final conclusion of the exercise solution.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises and Solutions (SGT8): Exercise 1: Nash Welfare Invariance Under Scaling
explanations.notes_quote: $(A_1, \ldots, A_n)$ still maximise Nash welfare

questions.source_ref: Section Exercises and Solutions (SGT8): Exercise 2: MMS Allocation When |G| <= |N| + 1
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the proof that an MMS allocation exists when $|G| \leq n+1$ and valuations are additive? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: If $|G| < n$, every partition has an empty bundle, so each agent's MMS is 0 and any allocation works
If $|G| = n$, any allocation giving each agent one item is MMS
If $|G| = n+1$, the proof lets $n-1$ agents sequentially pick favourite remaining items, leaving the last agent two items
The proof for $|G| = n+1$ shows the last agent necessarily gets utility exactly equal to their top-ranked item
The proof bounds the MMS by $\min\{v_i(g_{n-1}), v_i(g_n)+v_i(g_{n+1})\}$ in the $|G| = n+1$ case

explanations.text: This is the first case in the proof.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises and Solutions (SGT8): Exercise 2: MMS Allocation When |G| <= |N| + 1
explanations.notes_quote: If $|G| < n$: every partition has an empty bundle, so each agent's MMS = 0. Any allocation works

explanations.text: This is the second case in the proof.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises and Solutions (SGT8): Exercise 2: MMS Allocation When |G| <= |N| + 1
explanations.notes_quote: If $|G| = n$: MMS = value of least valuable item. Any allocation giving each agent one item is MMS

explanations.text: This is the constructive argument for the $n+1$ case.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises and Solutions (SGT8): Exercise 2: MMS Allocation When |G| <= |N| + 1
explanations.notes_quote: Let $n-1$ agents sequentially pick their favourite remaining item ... The last agent gets the remaining 2 items

explanations.text: The proof only guarantees the last agent gets two remaining items worth at least their MMS, not exactly their top-ranked item.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises and Solutions (SGT8): Exercise 2: MMS Allocation When |G| <= |N| + 1

explanations.text: This bound appears explicitly in the proof sketch.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises and Solutions (SGT8): Exercise 2: MMS Allocation When |G| <= |N| + 1
explanations.notes_quote: has MMS $= \min\{v_i(g_{n-1}), v_i(g_n) + v_i(g_{n+1})\}$

questions.source_ref: Section Exercises and Solutions (SGT8): Exercise 3: Round-Robin Guarantees 2/3 MMS
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the $\frac{2}{3}$-MMS guarantee proved for Round-Robin? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The assumptions include additive valuations, $v_i(G)=1$, and each item worth at most $1/(3n)$ to any agent
The proof uses the fact that RR outputs EF1
For each agent $i$ and each $j \neq i$, the proof derives $v_i(A_i) \geq v_i(A_j) - 1/(3n)$
Summing these inequalities yields $n \cdot v_i(A_i) \geq 2/3$, so $v_i(A_i) \geq 2/3$ for every agent
Since $\text{MMS}_i \leq 1/n$, the proof concludes $v_i(A_i) \geq \frac{2}{3} \cdot \text{MMS}_i$

explanations.text: These are the exact assumptions of the exercise.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises and Solutions (SGT8): Exercise 3: Round-Robin Guarantees 2/3 MMS
explanations.notes_quote: All agents have additive valuations, $v_i(G) = 1$, each item worth $\leq 1/(3n)$

explanations.text: The proof starts from RR's EF1 guarantee.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises and Solutions (SGT8): Exercise 3: Round-Robin Guarantees 2/3 MMS
explanations.notes_quote: RR outputs EF1

explanations.text: This is the key inequality used before summing over all $j$.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises and Solutions (SGT8): Exercise 3: Round-Robin Guarantees 2/3 MMS
explanations.notes_quote: For agent $i$ and each $j \neq i$: $v_i(A_i) \geq v_i(A_j) - 1/(3n)$

explanations.text: The proof gets $n \cdot v_i(A_i) \geq 2/3$, hence $v_i(A_i) \geq 2/(3n)$, not $2/3$.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises and Solutions (SGT8): Exercise 3: Round-Robin Guarantees 2/3 MMS

explanations.text: This is the final step of the argument.
explanations.section_ref: Section Exercises and Solutions (SGT8): Exercise 3: Round-Robin Guarantees 2/3 MMS
explanations.notes_quote: Thus $v_i(A_i) \geq \frac{2}{3} \cdot \text{MMS}_i$

questions.source_ref: Section Additional Concepts from LGT Notes: Maximin Share (MMS) Guarantee
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the maximin share (MMS) guarantee? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: $\text{MMS}_i = \max_{(X_1, \ldots, X_n)} \min_j v_i(X_j)$
The intuition is: agent $i$ partitions the goods into $n$ bundles and then receives the least valuable bundle under their own valuation
An MMS allocation guarantees $v_i(A_i) \geq \text{MMS}_i$ for all agents
For $n \geq 3$, additive valuations always admit an MMS allocation
Later work cited in the notes shows that allocations giving each player at least $3/4$ of their MMS can always be found

explanations.text: This is the formal definition given in the notes.
explanations.section_ref: Section Additional Concepts from LGT Notes: Maximin Share (MMS) Guarantee
explanations.notes_quote: $\text{MMS}_i = \max_{(X_1, \ldots, X_n)} \min_j v_i(X_j)$

explanations.text: The notes explain MMS using the cut-and-choose style intuition.
explanations.section_ref: Section Additional Concepts from LGT Notes: Maximin Share (MMS) Guarantee
explanations.notes_quote: What's the best I could do if I had to divide items into $n$ piles, knowing I'll get the least valuable pile?

explanations.text: This is the definition of an MMS allocation.
explanations.section_ref: Section Additional Concepts from LGT Notes: Maximin Share (MMS) Guarantee
explanations.notes_quote: An MMS allocation ensures $v_i(A_i) \geq \text{MMS}_i$ for all $i \in N$

explanations.text: The notes say the opposite: for $n \geq 3$, there are additive valuations with no MMS allocation.
explanations.section_ref: Section Additional Concepts from LGT Notes: Maximin Share (MMS) Guarantee

explanations.text: This is the approximation guarantee mentioned at the end of the section.
explanations.section_ref: Section Additional Concepts from LGT Notes: Maximin Share (MMS) Guarantee
explanations.notes_quote: Later work showed: can always find allocations giving each player $\geq 3/4$ of their MMS

questions.source_ref: Section Key Definitions
questions.text: Which of the following correctly match key fair-division terms with their definitions? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Monotone valuations: if $S \subseteq T$, then $v_i(S) \leq v_i(T)$
Envy graph: a directed graph with edge $i \to j$ if $i$ envies $j$
RAC: reallocation along a cycle in the envy graph
PROP1: removing one item from another agent's bundle eliminates envy
ECE: Envy-Cycle Elimination, which iteratively allocates to unenvied agents and uses RAC steps

explanations.text: This is the key-definition entry for monotone valuations.
explanations.notes_quote: Monotone valuations | $S \subseteq T \implies v_i(S) \leq v_i(T)$

explanations.text: This is the key-definition entry for envy graph.
explanations.notes_quote: Envy graph | Directed graph: edge $i \to j$ if $i$ envies $j$

explanations.text: This is the key-definition entry for RAC.
explanations.notes_quote: RAC | Reallocation along a cycle in the envy graph

explanations.text: That is EF1, not PROP1. PROP1 means adding one item to an agent's own bundle makes it proportional.
This is the key-definition entry for ECE.
explanations.notes_quote: ECE | Envy-Cycle Elimination: iteratively allocate to unenvied agents + RAC steps

questions.source_ref: Section Key Definitions
questions.text: Which of the following correctly match welfare and fairness terms from the key definitions table? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Utilitarian welfare: $UW(A) = \sum_{i \in N} v_i(A_i)$
Egalitarian welfare: $EW(A) = \max_{i \in N} v_i(A_i)$
Nash welfare: $NW(A) = \prod_{i \in N} v_i(A_i)$
EFX: removing any item from $A_j$ eliminates $i$'s envy of $j$
MMS: $\max_{(X_1,\ldots,X_n)} \min_j v_i(X_j)$, the best guarantee under adversarial choice

explanations.text: This is the key-definition entry for utilitarian welfare.
explanations.notes_quote: Utilitarian welfare | $UW(A) = \sum_{i \in N} v_i(A_i)$

explanations.text: Egalitarian welfare is the minimum utility, not the maximum.
This is the key-definition entry for Nash welfare.
explanations.notes_quote: Nash welfare | $NW(A) = \prod_{i \in N} v_i(A_i)$

explanations.text: This is the key-definition entry for EFX.
explanations.notes_quote: EFX | Removing any item from $A_j$ eliminates $i$'s envy of $j$

explanations.text: This is the key-definition entry for MMS, including the adversarial-choice intuition.
explanations.notes_quote: MMS | $\max_{(X_1,\ldots,X_n)} \min_j v_i(X_j)$ -- best guarantee under adversarial choice

questions.source_ref: Learning Objectives; Section 8.2: PROP1 vs. EF1; Section 8.3: Theorem: Round-Robin Outputs EF1 (and hence PROP1) for Additive Valuations
questions.text: Which of the following integrative statements correctly connect fairness notions and algorithmic guarantees from Week 8? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: For additive valuations, a complete EF1 allocation is also PROP1
Round-Robin provides an EF1 allocation for additive valuations, and therefore also a PROP1 allocation
ECE provides EF1 for monotone valuations by alternating between allocating to unenvied agents and performing RAC steps
Exact envy-freeness is always achievable for indivisible goods once valuations are additive
Week 8 presents EF1 as a relaxation that is algorithmically achievable even when exact fairness is infeasible

explanations.text: This is one of the central theoretical results of the week.
explanations.notes_quote: YES for additive valuations

explanations.text: The RR theorem states EF1, and the section title explicitly adds 'and hence PROP1'.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.3: Theorem: Round-Robin Outputs EF1 (and hence PROP1) for Additive Valuations
explanations.notes_quote: Round-Robin Outputs EF1 (and hence PROP1) for Additive Valuations

explanations.text: This summarises the ECE algorithm correctly.
explanations.notes_quote: If there is an unenvied agent, allocate them an unallocated item ... Otherwise, there must be an envy cycle -- perform an RAC step

explanations.text: The notes say exact envy-freeness is generally infeasible for indivisible goods; additivity does not remove that impossibility in general.
This is the overall conceptual arc of the week.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.2: The Core Challenge; Section 8.2: Relaxations: PROP1 and EF1
explanations.notes_quote: With indivisible goods, envy-freeness and proportionality are generally infeasible ... Relaxations: PROP1 and EF1

questions.source_ref: Learning Objectives; Section 8.5: Properties of Welfare Maximisers; Section 8.6: Existence of EFX (Open Problem); Section Additional Concepts from LGT Notes: Maximin Share (MMS) Guarantee
questions.text: Which of the following integrative statements correctly summarise the limits and open problems discussed in Week 8? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Neither Round-Robin nor ECE guarantees Pareto-optimality in general
For additive valuations, Nash welfare maximisation is highlighted as achieving both PO and EF1
The existence of EFX for $n \geq 4$ agents with additive valuations remains open
For $n \geq 3$, exact MMS allocations are always guaranteed for additive valuations
Even though exact MMS allocations may fail to exist for $n \geq 3$, the notes mention universal $3/4$-MMS approximations

explanations.text: Both algorithms are shown to have non-PO counterexamples.
explanations.section_ref: Section 8.3: Limitations of Round-Robin; Section 8.4: ECE Is Also Not Pareto-Optimal
explanations.notes_quote: Not Pareto-optimal ... ECE Is Also Not Pareto-Optimal

explanations.text: This is one of the main positive results in the welfare section.
explanations.notes_quote: Nash welfare maximiser gives both PO and EF1 (for additive valuations)

explanations.text: This is the major open problem highlighted in the EFX section.
explanations.notes_quote: $n \geq 4$ agents, additive valuations | Open problem

explanations.text: The notes say the opposite: for $n \geq 3$, there are additive valuations with no MMS allocation.
explanations.section_ref: Section Additional Concepts from LGT Notes: Maximin Share (MMS) Guarantee

explanations.text: This is the approximation guarantee mentioned in the MMS discussion.
explanations.section_ref: Section Additional Concepts from LGT Notes: Maximin Share (MMS) Guarantee
explanations.notes_quote: can always find allocations giving each player $\geq 3/4$ of their MMS
